doc.title: Diluted EPS — convertible bonds (if-converted)
doc.event: Hale Corp.: income available to common $490,000, WASO 200,000 (basic EPS $2.45). Outstanding: $500,000 of 6% convertible bonds, convertible into 25,000 shares; tax rate 20%. Compute diluted EPS.

computationPaths.narration: If-converted method: had the bonds been stock all year, the company would NOT have paid the 30,000 interest — but it also would have paid tax on that income. So the numerator adds back interest × (1 − tax rate), and the denominator adds the conversion shares. Both adjustments, always together.

memorize.body: If-converted: Diluted EPS = (income to common + interest × (1 − tax)) ÷ (WASO + conversion shares). Convertible PREFERRED: add back the preferred dividend instead — NO tax adjustment (dividends aren't deductible).

ANTIDILUTION check: if the pretend-conversion RAISES EPS above basic, exclude that security — diluted EPS only reports the worst honest case. Test each convertible separately (most dilutive first when there are several).
Bonds issued mid-year? Weight both adjustments — interest actually incurred and shares × months/12 — the same clock WASO runs on.

questions.prompt: Income to common 490,000; WASO 200,000 (basic $2.45). Convertible bonds: 500,000 face, 6%, convertible into 25,000 shares; tax rate 20%. Diluted EPS =

distractors.feedback: 2.31 adds back the full 30,000 PRE-tax interest. Avoiding the interest would have raised taxable income — only the after-tax 24,000 comes back: 514,000 ÷ 225,000 = 2.28.
2.18 adds the 25,000 shares but forgets the numerator add-back (490,000 ÷ 225,000). If-converted always adjusts BOTH: the interest returns to income when the shares join the count.
